content:
**March 18, 2026 — Washington, D.C.**

In a widely anticipated move, the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) voted on Wednesday to keep the target range for the federal funds rate unchanged at 4.75% to 5.00%. The decision marks the seventh consecutive meeting at which the committee has maintained the upper bound at 5.00%, signaling a continued wait-and-see approach in the face of mixed economic signals.

Fed Chair Lisa Cook, speaking at the post-meeting press conference, emphasized the need for “greater clarity on inflation’s trajectory” before considering any rate cuts. “While inflation has retreated from its 2022 peaks, core prices remain above our 2% target, and the labor market is showing signs of resilience,” Cook said. “We are committed to achieving our dual mandate, and will adjust policy as warranted by incoming data.”

Market participants had largely priced in a hold, following recent CPI releases showing annual inflation at 2.6%. The Fed’s statement noted “modest progress” toward price stability but cited “persistent uncertainties” in global supply chains and energy markets.

Economists expect the Fed to maintain its cautious stance through at least mid-2026, with futures markets now pushing back expectations for the first rate cut until late summer.

“The Fed’s patience is justified,” said Morgan Stanley’s chief U.S. economist, Emily Chen. “They want to avoid reigniting inflation, especially in an election year.”

The next FOMC meeting is scheduled for May 6–7.
